translation: For the accustomed security of the Quraysh
commentary:
لِإِيلَـٰفِ قُرَيْشٍ

This surah follows immediately after Surah Al-Fil, and the connection between them is profound. While the structure is brief, we can examine it in two distinct parts. The first part details what Allah ﷻ does for the people of Quraysh—the specific favors He grants them for their convenience and prosperity.

The opening establishes that everything being described is connected to a divine purpose. Allah ﷻ is explaining why He protected this tribe, why He granted them these unique privileges among all the Arabian tribes.

translation: Their accustomed security [in] the caravan of winter and summer
commentary:
إِۦلَـٰفِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ ٱلشِّتَآءِ وَٱلصَّيْفِ

Allah ﷻ has opened the travel routes for Quraysh in a remarkable way. He enabled them to journey during both summer and winter—the most convenient seasons for trade. Even more significantly, they could travel without fear of being robbed or attacked by other tribes, despite the fact that highway robbery was common throughout Arabia at that time. 

Why did they enjoy this unprecedented security? Because they were the custodians of the Sacred House. Their position as guardians of the Kaaba granted them a protected status that no other tribe possessed. This safety wasn't due to their own power or military might—it was a direct blessing from Allah ﷻ.

translation: Let them worship the Lord of this House
commentary:
فَلْيَعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَيْتِ

Now comes the pivotal question: Why did Allah ﷻ grant them all these conveniences? Why did He protect them against the Army of the Elephant in the previous surah? For what purpose? The answer is direct: they should worship the Master of this House.

Notice the specific language used here. Allah ﷻ doesn't simply say "they should worship Allah." Instead, He says they should worship رَبَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَيْتِ—the Lord of this House. This phrasing carries deep significance. The House was built by Prophet Ibrahim (AS) for one explicit purpose: the worship of One God alone.

This language poses a powerful challenge to Quraysh: Look at what you've done with that House. How could you enjoy all the benefits that came to you as custodians of this House, yet simultaneously violate its very sanctity? How could you fill it with idols when it was established for the worship of Allah ﷻ alone?

translation: Who has fed them, [saving them] from hunger and made them safe, [saving them] from fear
commentary:
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَطْعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٍ وَءَامَنَهُم مِّنْ خَوْفٍۭ

This final ayah makes the connection complete. Allah ﷻ is the One who fed them after hunger and gave them security after fear. This is actually the fulfillment of Prophet Ibrahim's (AS) prayer for Makkah.

When we look back at Ibrahim's (AS) supplication, we find two key requests: first, that Allah ﷻ make this land safe—وَاجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا آمِنًا (Make this a secure city). Second, that He provide its people with provisions and all kinds of fruits. Surah Al-Fil demonstrated how Allah ﷻ fulfilled the first part—establishing safety. Now Surah Quraysh shows how He fulfilled the second part—granting prosperity through their ability to travel and trade freely.

This pairing of peace and prosperity is essential. These two blessings must go hand in hand for a society to truly survive and flourish. Peace without prosperity means security but starvation—what good is a safe house if you cannot find work to feed your family? Prosperity without peace means wealth but constant fear—what value is there in having resources if your home, your business and your life are under threat?

Allah ﷻ granted both of these fundamental blessings to Quraysh. He gave them peace in Surah Al-Fil and prosperity in Surah Quraysh. They could survive, thrive and flourish as a society in ways no other Arabian tribe could.

And in return, He asked for one thing: worship the Master of this House. Restore the legacy of Ibrahim (AS). Return to the pure monotheism for which the Kaaba was originally built. That was the only requirement—yet it was the one thing they failed to fulfill.
